metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace AC5's process language with verifiable repo artifacts: specify (a) that the ADR input is committed as a named stub/section in `docs/adr/` (even if 2eca authors the final file) and (b) that the follow-on ticket URL is recorded in a `DECISIONS.md` or PR description field, then name the proving command (e.g., `grep -r 'async-watchdog' docs/adr/` or a CI link check).
findings.finding: AC5 uses effort/process language ('provides the content for', 'files the discovered_from follow-on ticket') rather than specifying a verifiable repo artifact; no proving command exists to confirm these outcomes in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'This story provides the content for the deferred-async-watchdog ADR... and files the discovered_from follow-on ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC5 with: 'A stub entry for the async-watchdog ADR (containing the architectural rationale captured in the What/Scope sections) is committed to `docs/adr/` and a follow-on ticket URL is recorded in `DECISIONS.md`; verified by `grep -r "stream-idle-watchdog" docs/adr/ && grep "follow-on" DECISIONS.md`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new timeout/step-cap config keys belong on `LLMConfig` (consistent with `timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations` and 95a5's pattern) rather than `VerifyConfig`, and update the Scope section accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan places all new config keys under `verify.*` (VerifyConfig) — `verify.llm_request_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_tool_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit`, `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit` — but this contradicts a verifiable codebase pattern: the existing per-request LLM operational knobs (`timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`) all live on `LLMConfig`, not `VerifyConfig`. A grep of `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` shows zero LLM operational knobs there. Moreover, sibling dependency ticket 95a5 (which this story builds on) explicitly states: 'every existing LLM OPERATIONAL knob (`timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`) already lives on `LLMConfig` … So add fields + `from_env` lines using `_llm_int(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_RETRY_*", …)` read from the `[llm]` table.' Placing this story's knobs in `VerifyConfig` instead would create an inconsistency with the established pattern and with 95a5's `LLMConfig`-based construction helper.
findings.location: Scope section: 'config keys with concrete defaults: `verify.llm_request_timeout_s` (default 600)…`verify.llm_tool_timeout_s`…`verify.llm_agentic_request_limit`…`verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the four new keys to `LLMConfig` (e.g. `llm_request_timeout_s`, `llm_tool_timeout_s`, `llm_agentic_request_limit`, `llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`) with `REBAR_LLM_*` env vars and `_llm_int` wiring, matching the established pattern and 95a5's sibling config keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Relocate the four new config keys (llm_request_timeout_s, llm_tool_timeout_s, llm_agentic_request_limit, llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit) from VerifyConfig/verify.* to LLMConfig in src/rebar/llm/config.py (mirroring timeout_s, max_iterations) so they are consistent with the 95a5-established pattern and the _build_retrying_anthropic_model merge interface.
findings.finding: The four new config keys are placed under the 'verify.*' section (VerifyConfig / _config_schema.py), but every existing LLM operational/runtime knob — timeout_s, max_tokens, max_iterations, plus 95a5's llm_retry_max_attempts/llm_retry_max_wait_s — lives on LLMConfig in src/rebar/llm/config.py, read from the [llm] table. VerifyConfig holds gate semantic toggles, not model-runtime knobs. Placing these in VerifyConfig contradicts the established pattern, would also contradict the 95a5-defined merge interface (which threads httpx.Timeout from cfg: LLMConfig), and adds a cross-section dependency that doesn't exist anywhere else.
findings.location: Scope section: 'config keys with concrete defaults: verify.llm_request_timeout_s ... verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit'; src/rebar/_config_schema.py VerifyConfig; src/rebar/llm/config.py LLMConfig
findings.suggested_fix: Add the four fields to LLMConfig as llm_request_timeout_s (default 600), llm_tool_timeout_s (default 120), llm_agentic_request_limit (default 60), llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit (default 200), resolved via _llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_S', ...) in LLMConfig.from_env — exactly mirroring timeout_s and the 95a5 llm_retry_* additions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the four new config keys (`llm_request_timeout_s`, `llm_tool_timeout_s`, `llm_agentic_request_limit`, `llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`) from the `verify.*` namespace to `LLMConfig` (in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`) with `REBAR_LLM_*` env vars and `_llm_int` resolution — consistent with every existing LLM runtime knob and the dep story 95a5's explicit rule.
findings.finding: The plan places the four new config knobs under the `verify.*` namespace (e.g. `verify.llm_request_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit`), but every analogous LLM runtime knob in the codebase (`timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`) lives on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` with `REBAR_LLM_*` env vars. The dependency story 95a5 explicitly codifies this rule: 'every existing LLM OPERATIONAL knob already lives on LLMConfig; [verify] holds verification-GATE semantic toggles, not model-runtime knobs.' Placing the new timeouts in `VerifyConfig` means the runner — which reads `LLMConfig`, not `VerifyConfig` — will not see them, breaking the wiring without an error.
findings.location: Scope section — 'config keys with concrete defaults: verify.llm_request_timeout_s ... verify.llm_tool_timeout_s ... verify.llm_agentic_request_limit ... verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit'; Files: src/rebar/config.py, src/rebar/_config_schema.py
findings.suggested_fix: In `LLMConfig` add: `llm_request_timeout_s: int = 600`, `llm_tool_timeout_s: int = 120`, `llm_agentic_request_limit: int = 60`, `llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit: int = 200`. In `LLMConfig.from_env()` resolve them via `_llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_S', 'llm_request_timeout_s', 600)` etc., matching the existing pattern. Remove references to `verify.llm_*` from the Scope and Files sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the reconciliation between the new `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` and the existing `model_settings['timeout']`: specify whether `model_settings['timeout']` is retained (belt-and-suspenders), replaced by the finer-grained httpx.Timeout, or superseded only on the Anthropic path — so the implementer does not inadvertently leave two conflicting timeout signals on the same request.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the new `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` object relates to the existing `model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s)` that is already wired at runner.py:285-286. Both derive from overlapping semantics (request timeout), but they operate at different layers (httpx per-read vs SDK total request). The dep story 95a5 explicitly flags this as a 'benign overlap that story hoopoe/blackbear reconcile' — meaning this story is responsible for stating the reconciliation. Without it, the implementer may end up with two conflicting timeout signals on the same request, or may inadvertently preserve the coarser model_settings timeout that this plan is superseding.
findings.location: Scope section — 'the read timeout is set as an httpx.Timeout on the SAME httpx.AsyncClient that story 95a5 constructs'; What section — 'a generous but finite per-request read timeout via httpx.Timeout(read=...)'; dependency 95a5 comment: 'the pre-existing model_settings['timeout']... a benign overlap that story hoopoe/blackbear reconcile'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the provider scope of the `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` wiring: if it is Anthropic-only (via 95a5's construction seam), say so explicitly and either document the OpenAI/Gemini gap as a known limitation or describe a parallel wiring mechanism for those providers.
findings.finding: The plan states 'a generous per-request read timeout is set for both modes (as httpx.Timeout)' — but the httpx.AsyncClient carrying the timeout is contributed to 95a5's `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` helper, which is Anthropic-path-only (runner.py:233: `if self._model_override is None and resolved.startswith('anthropic')`). OpenAI and Gemini paths build the model from a plain provider-qualified string and never pass through that construction, so they would not receive the `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` at all. The AC similarly only proves the mechanism under a `FunctionModel`, which is not provider-specific.
findings.location: Scope section — 'the read timeout is set as an httpx.Timeout on the SAME httpx.AsyncClient that story 95a5 constructs (95a5 owns that construction seam)'; AC1 — 'a generous per-request read timeout is set for both modes (as httpx.Timeout)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the FunctionModel-sleep probe with a mock HTTP server (e.g., a local server that accepts a TCP connection and sends no bytes) to confirm that `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` wired to the shared `AsyncClient` actually fires and interrupts `agent.run_sync()` — matching the same approach story 95a5 used for its transport-layer spike.
findings.finding: The plan's probe to verify 'httpx read timeout fires under run_sync' uses a FunctionModel that sleeps — but FunctionModel is pydantic-ai's in-process test double that never creates an httpx request. The httpx.Timeout(read=...) only fires when the transport waits for bytes from a live socket; a sleeping in-process function times out via coroutine/task cancellation, not via the httpx read-timeout path. The capability assertion 'httpx read timeout fires under run_sync's internally-managed event loop' is therefore unverified by the named probe.
findings.location: Scope section — 'own timeout probe' paragraph: 'a FunctionModel that sleeps past the read timeout asserts the call is interrupted+classified under run_sync'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `pytest-httpserver` or a simple `socketserver.TCPServer` that accepts a connection but never sends bytes; wire the `httpx.AsyncClient` with `httpx.Timeout(read=<short_s>)` to it, run `agent.run_sync()`, and assert a timeout exception is raised. This mirrors 95a5's 429→200 spike approach and actually exercises the httpx boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why the new timeout/step-cap config keys belong in `verify.*` (VerifyConfig) rather than `LLMConfig`, given that story 95a5 explicitly established LLMConfig as the home for LLM operational knobs — or move them to LLMConfig to match the established pattern.
findings.finding: The plan places new LLM-operational timeout config keys (`verify.llm_request_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_tool_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit`, `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`) in `VerifyConfig` (the core schema in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`), but every analogous existing LLM operational knob (`timeout_s`, `max_iterations`, `max_tokens`) already lives in `LLMConfig` (`src/rebar/llm/config.py`). The prior story 95a5 explicitly resolved this placement question and put its retry keys in `LLMConfig`. The plan does not justify why these timeout/step-cap knobs belong in the core schema's `VerifyConfig` rather than `LLMConfig`.
findings.location: Scope section: 'config keys with concrete defaults: `verify.llm_request_timeout_s` (default 600), `verify.llm_tool_timeout_s` (default 120), `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit` (default 60), `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit` (default 200)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the four new config keys (`llm_request_timeout_s`, `llm_tool_timeout_s`, `llm_agentic_request_limit`, `llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`) belong on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` (not `VerifyConfig`/`_config_schema.py`), consistent with all existing LLM runtime knobs and story 95a5's explicit rationale.
findings.finding: The four new config keys are placed in the `verify.*` namespace in the plan's Scope section, but `VerifyConfig` (in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`) holds gate-semantic toggles (booleans, thresholds), not LLM runtime operational knobs. All analogous runtime knobs (`timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`, and story 95a5's `llm_retry_max_attempts`/`llm_retry_max_wait_s`) live on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`. No `llm_*` keys exist in `_SECTIONS['verify']` or `VerifyConfig`. Placing the new keys under `verify.*` would make them unreachable from `PydanticAIRunner.run()` which receives an `LLMConfig`, not a `VerifyConfig`.
findings.location: Scope section — config keys `verify.llm_request_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_tool_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit`, `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace `verify.llm_request_timeout_s` → `llm_request_timeout_s` (on `LLMConfig`, `[llm]` table, env `REBAR_LLM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_S`), and likewise for the other three keys. Update `src/rebar/llm/config.py:from_env` and `docs/config.md` to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the reconciliation rule between the new `httpx.Timeout(read=llm_request_timeout_s)` and the existing `model_settings['timeout']` path at `runner.py:285-286`: state whether the old path is removed (the new path supersedes it), both are kept (and why that's safe), or the old path is updated to derive from the new config key.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `verify.llm_request_timeout_s` (default 600) as a new `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` to be set on the shared client, but `runner.py:285-286` already sets `model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s)` using the existing `LLMConfig.timeout_s` (also default 600). Story 95a5 notes this is a 'benign overlap that story hoopoe/blackbear reconcile' — but this plan (hoopoe) never specifies the reconciliation rule: does the new `httpx.Timeout` replace the `model_settings['timeout']` path, co-exist with it, or does the old path get removed?
findings.location: Scope section — 'Shared-client coordination'; runner.py lines 279-286

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand the Scope probe) requiring a mock HTTP server that accepts a TCP connection and then sends no bytes, confirming that the httpx.Timeout(read=...) actually fires and the call is classified as a timeout failure — so the AsyncClient wiring is verified at the httpx boundary, not only through an in-process FunctionModel substitute.
findings.finding: The AC for 'hung model request aborted by read timeout' (AC3) and the Scope probe both use a FunctionModel (in-process function, no HTTP layer) to verify that the httpx.Timeout(read=...) fires — but a FunctionModel never goes through the httpx client, so this test cannot exercise or validate that the timeout is correctly wired to the shared AsyncClient. The new default behavior (httpx read timeout fires on a slow real HTTP response) is validated solely through an in-process substitute that bypasses the httpx boundary entirely; the wiring could be completely absent or misconfigured and the probe would still pass.
findings.location: Scope (own timeout probe paragraph) and AC3: 'A hung model request (no bytes past the read timeout) is aborted and classified'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a probe using a real (loopback) HTTP server fixture (e.g., a simple asyncio TCP server or `pytest-httpserver` in 'hang' mode) that accepts the connection and writes nothing. Run `agent.run_sync()` with the wired client pointing at this server; assert the call raises/classifies within ~read_timeout+margin seconds. This proves httpx.Timeout(read=...) is actually wired to the AsyncClient used by the agent, which a FunctionModel probe cannot.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the config-key namespace conflict: either move `llm_request_timeout_s`, `llm_tool_timeout_s`, `llm_agentic_request_limit`, `llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit` to `LLMConfig` (consistent with all existing LLM runtime knobs and 95a5's explicit rationale), or explicitly specify the cross-layer plumbing path that carries `VerifyConfig` values into the `_build_retrying_anthropic_model()` helper without coupling the LLM layer to the gate config layer.
findings.finding: Config keys named in Scope as 'verify.llm_request_timeout_s / verify.llm_tool_timeout_s / verify.llm_agentic_request_limit / verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit' (VerifyConfig / _config_schema.py) but the httpx.Timeout object they produce must reach 95a5's _build_retrying_anthropic_model() helper in the LLM layer (rebar/llm/). The existing architecture and 95a5's explicit rationale place ALL LLM-runtime operational knobs on LLMConfig (rebar/llm/config.py), not VerifyConfig, to avoid importing VerifyConfig into the LLM layer. The plan gives no plumbing path for how a VerifyConfig value crosses into the LLM layer's client constructor — creating a feasibility gap for the config-to-client wiring.
findings.location: Scope section — 'config keys with concrete defaults: verify.llm_request_timeout_s … verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit'; Files section — src/rebar/config.py, src/rebar/_config_schema.py
findings.suggested_fix: Follow 95a5's established pattern: add the four keys to LLMConfig in src/rebar/llm/config.py with REBAR_LLM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_S / REBAR_LLM_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S / REBAR_LLM_AGENTIC_REQUEST_LIMIT / REBAR_LLM_AGENTIC_TOOL_CALLS_LIMIT env vars and [llm] table keys, using _llm_int(). Remove the verify.* prefix from the Scope and Files sections. The 95a5 helper signature already accepts http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None — build the httpx.Timeout from LLMConfig values in the same construction helper.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the proving strategy for the 'no total-runtime timeout' absence claim in AC3: either a grep/AST-based test (with the exact pattern, e.g., `grep -rn 'timeout.*total\|signal\.alarm\|threading\.Timer' src/rebar/llm/runner.py`) that would catch a re-introduction, or a behavioral probe that runs long enough to confirm no interrupt, with the test file/function named.
findings.finding: AC3's negative assertion ('no total-runtime timeout exists anywhere in the gate path — asserted by test') does not describe how a test proves an absence; no proving command or test strategy is given for the absence claim.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'A hung model request... is aborted and classified; no total-runtime timeout exists anywhere in the gate path (asserted by test).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Verified by a static assertion in `tests/llm/test_runner_liveness.py` that `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` contains no reference to a total-runtime cap pattern: `assert not re.search(r"total.timeout|signal\.alarm", open("src/rebar/llm/runner.py").read())`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete artifact this story produces for the ADR hand-off to 2eca (e.g., a `docs/adr/00XX-deferred-async-watchdog.md` stub with Context/Decision/Consequences drafted, or a specific ticket comment format), so 2eca's integration step has a defined input to record.
findings.finding: The plan states 'this story provides the content for the deferred-async-watchdog ADR (RECORDED by story 2eca, the sole ADR owner — not authored independently here)' and AC item 5 says 'This story provides the content ... and files the discovered_from follow-on ticket.' The mechanism for transferring 'content' from this story to 2eca is unspecified: does the executor write a draft file, add a ticket comment, embed it in the commit message? Story 2eca's own description confirms it 'records' the ADR but says 'content authored by the noted stories' — creating an ambiguous hand-off with no concrete artifact.
findings.location: AC item 5 — 'This story provides the content for the deferred-async-watchdog ADR (RECORDED by story 2eca)'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the pattern established by story 95a5: write an ADR stub file at a placeholder path (e.g., `docs/adr/00XX-deferred-async-watchdog.md`) with real content (not an empty file), noting that 2eca assigns the final number at integration — matching exactly what 95a5 did for `docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement in Scope/Files that this story's httpx.Timeout object is contributed via the 95a5-defined _build_retrying_anthropic_model helper's http_timeout parameter (in src/rebar/llm/runner.py), and confirm that the existing model_settings['timeout'] path at runner.py:285-286 is reconciled or replaced.
findings.finding: The plan does not list src/rebar/llm/runner.py's _build_retrying_anthropic_model helper (defined by story 95a5) as a named consumer/edit target that this story contributes into. The 95a5 ticket defines the concrete merge interface: '_build_retrying_anthropic_model(name, *, base_url, cfg, http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = None)' — this story must pass its httpx.Timeout object into that helper. Without naming this seam in Scope/Files, an implementer may wire the timeout via the pre-existing model_settings['timeout'] path (runner.py:285-286) rather than the 95a5 client-construction seam, creating the duplicate/inconsistent timeout-layer overlap that 95a5 explicitly flags as needing reconciliation.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Shared-client coordination'; 95a5 ticket description: '_build_retrying_anthropic_model(name, *, base_url, cfg, http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = None)'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the ADR authorship contradiction: either remove 'docs/adr/' from the Files list and strike the unqualified 'Capture the decision...in an ADR' phrase from the What section (deferring entirely to story 2eca), or explicitly clarify that this story produces a DRAFT/input document (not the final ADR file) that 2eca incorporates.
findings.finding: The Files section lists 'docs/adr/' as a file this story touches, and the What section says 'Capture the decision + the async-migration trigger in an ADR' — but AC5 explicitly says the ADR is 'RECORDED by story 2eca, the sole ADR owner — not authored independently here.' An implementer reading the Files list and What body text would create an ADR file; reading AC5 they would not. Two sections send the implementer in opposite directions.
findings.location: Files section ('docs/adr/'); What section (Deferred paragraph); AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In the What/Deferred paragraph, change 'Capture the decision + the async-migration trigger in an ADR' to 'Provide written content (decision rationale + async-migration trigger) for story 2eca to record in the ADR — do not create an ADR file directly.' Remove 'docs/adr/' from the Files list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'docs/adr/' from the Files section (since AC5 delegates all ADR authorship to story 2eca) OR replace it with the specific ADR file path that this story is expected to touch (e.g. a stub), reconciling the Files list with AC5's explicit delegation.
findings.finding: The plan's Files section lists 'docs/adr/' as an edit target for this story, but AC5 explicitly says the deferred-async-watchdog ADR is 'RECORDED by story 2eca, the sole ADR owner — not authored independently here.' This is a contradictory scope signal: if the story genuinely does not author any ADR content, docs/adr/ should not appear in Files; if it contributes ADR content (e.g. a stub), that contradicts AC5.
findings.location: Files section: 'docs/adr/'; AC5: 'RECORDED by story 2eca, the sole ADR owner — not authored independently here'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the classification protocol for timeout exceptions: specify which exception type(s) a hung-model read-timeout and a hung-tool tool-timeout surface as (e.g., `httpx.ReadTimeout` → `LLMUnavailableError` with `resolution_class=WAIT_AND_RETRY`), and confirm this is compatible with story 2eca/mamba's `resolution_class` enum mapping.
findings.finding: The plan's AC states that a 'hung model request' is 'aborted and classified' and a 'hung tool trips the tool timeout' where 'each surfaces a classified failure', but the plan never defines the classification protocol: which exception type the timeout surfaces as (raw `httpx.ReadTimeout`, re-raised as `LLMUnavailableError`, or a new distinct exception), and — critically — which `resolution_class` enum value (owned by story 2eca/mamba, e.g. `WAIT_AND_RETRY`, `RETRY_NOW`) a timeout maps to. The existing `runner.py:353-365` catch-all re-wraps all non-LLMError exceptions as `LLMUnavailableError`, which the plan may or may not intend as sufficient 'classification'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria items 1, 2, 3 — 'classified failure', 'aborted and classified'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a defined contingency to the probe: if the `FunctionModel`-sleep probe shows the `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` does NOT fire under `run_sync()`, specify the fallback design (e.g., escalate to async migration, use a thread-based watchdog, or raise a blocker against 2eca).
findings.finding: The plan requires 'its own small probe' (a FunctionModel that sleeps past the read timeout and a sleeping tool) to confirm the timeout mechanism fires under `run_sync()`, and states 'confirming the timeout mechanism is run_sync-compatible before the design is relied upon.' No fallback design is specified for the case where the probe demonstrates that `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` on the async client does NOT interrupt `agent.run_sync()`'s internally-managed event loop — the probe is presented as a necessary precondition but without a contingency.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Own timeout probe (distinct from the transport spike)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the explicit interface by which this story contributes the read timeout to 95a5's shared AsyncClient (e.g., 95a5 accepts a `timeout: httpx.Timeout` parameter at client-construction time sourced from config; this story owns the config keys and passes the value at construction) so the ownership boundary and atomicity are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The mechanism by which this story 'contributes' the httpx read timeout into story 95a5's shared AsyncClient is not specified — it is unclear whether this is a constructor-time config injection, a post-construction mutation, or an interface contract — leaving the ownership boundary and atomicity of the contribution undefined.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Shared-client coordination' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Specify in the Scope section: '95a5's client constructor accepts an `httpx.Timeout` argument; this story adds the config keys and passes `httpx.Timeout(read=verify.llm_request_timeout_s, connect=10)` at the single construction call-site. No post-construction mutation of the client occurs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Unify the story identifier: replace 'arcticduck' in the experiment log with '95a5' (or add a parenthetical '(story 95a5, a.k.a. arcticduck)') so one name is used consistently.
findings.finding: The shared-client story is referred to as '95a5' throughout the plan body but is called 'arcticduck' in the Verified library behavior experiment log ('owned by story arcticduck'). These appear to be the same story (the one that constructs the shared httpx.AsyncClient), but no cross-reference confirms this, leaving a terminology inconsistency.
findings.location: Scope section ('story 95a5'); Verified library behavior log ('owned by story arcticduck')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Verified library behavior log, change 'owned by story arcticduck' to 'owned by story 95a5'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify the title to reflect deferral, e.g. 'Activity-based liveness: per-request timeout + step caps (idle-watchdog deferred — see ADR)', so the title and body agree on what ships.
findings.finding: The plan title lists 'idle-watchdog' as a first-class peer deliverable but the body defers it entirely; a reader scanning only the title would expect the watchdog to ship with this story.
findings.location: Title / What section / AC (no AC for idle-watchdog delivery)
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Activity-based liveness: per-request timeout + step caps + idle-watchdog ADR (no total-runtime timeout)' — making clear the watchdog's only artifact here is the ADR entry, not working code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the four proposed LLM runtime config keys (`llm_request_timeout_s`, `llm_tool_timeout_s`, `llm_agentic_request_limit`, `llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`) from `VerifyConfig`/`[tool.rebar.verify]` to `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` with `REBAR_LLM_*` env vars, matching the existing pattern used by `timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`, and (per story 95a5) `llm_retry_max_attempts`.
findings.finding: The plan places four new LLM runtime knobs (`verify.llm_request_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_tool_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit`, `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`) in `VerifyConfig` / `[tool.rebar.verify]`, contradicting the codebase's established and enforced pattern where every LLM runtime knob lives on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` with a `REBAR_LLM_*` env var.
findings.location: Scope section: `verify.llm_request_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_tool_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit`, `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit`; Files: `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `src/rebar/llm/config.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `LLMConfig`: `llm_request_timeout_s: int = 600`, `llm_tool_timeout_s: int = 120`, `llm_agentic_request_limit: int = 60`, `llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit: int = 200`; wire each with `_llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_S', 'llm_request_timeout_s', 600)` etc. in `from_env`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit` / `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit` are genuinely distinct from the existing `REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` / `cfg.max_iterations` mechanism, or whether the existing per-op floor pattern (used in `completion.py:306` and `operations.py:138`) already covers the agentic-call step-cap need without new config keys.
findings.finding: The plan's new `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit` (default 60) and `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit` (default 200) duplicate config surface that already exists as `REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` / `cfg.max_iterations`, from which the runner already derives `request_limit` and `tool_calls_limit` — adding two new knobs for the same mechanism is config proliferation.
findings.location: Scope section: `verify.llm_agentic_request_limit` (default 60), `verify.llm_agentic_tool_calls_limit` (default 200); `src/rebar/llm/runner.py:310-313`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC explicitly verifying that `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` (or whatever 95a5's construction helper is named) accepts and applies the `http_timeout: httpx.Timeout` parameter, so the integration contract is pinned before this story's implementation begins.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '95a5 constructs [the `httpx.AsyncClient`]' and 'this story CONTRIBUTES the timeout into 95a5's client … concrete interface: `_build_retrying_anthropic_model(name, *, base_url, cfg, http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = None)`'. A grep of the entire `src/rebar` tree shows zero occurrences of `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`, `httpx.AsyncClient`, or `httpx.Timeout`. The current `runner.py` still uses the pre-95a5 construction path (direct `AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct)` for the loopback branch, SDK-internal provider for the normal path). The plan's timeout injection mechanism depends on this helper existing at merge time, but the interface is only described in 95a5's ticket — not yet landed in the repo snapshot.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Shared-client coordination … this story CONTRIBUTES the timeout into 95a5's client … concrete interface: `_build_retrying_anthropic_model(name, *, base_url, cfg, http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = None)`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the `run_sync` line-number citations in the Why section from `323,461,474` to the current `327,465,478` (or replace with a grep-stable reference).
findings.finding: The plan cites `src/rebar/llm/runner.py:323,461,474` as the three `run_sync` call sites. A read of the file shows the actual line numbers are 327, 465, and 478. While the assertion that `run_sync` is used exclusively is correct, the specific line-number citations in the plan are stale and will mislead implementors doing targeted edits.
findings.location: Why section: 'the runner uses `agent.run_sync()` exclusively (`src/rebar/llm/runner.py:323,461,474`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with `agent.run_sync` (three call sites — use `grep -n run_sync src/rebar/llm/runner.py` to locate them at implementation time) to avoid stale line-number references.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add tests/unit/test_config_llm.py (and docs/env-vars.md if a drift gate regenerates it) to the Files section, confirming that the _clean fixture will include the new env var names for the four new config keys.
findings.finding: The plan does not list tests/unit/test_config_llm.py as a file that needs updating, but story 95a5's AC explicitly requires that its new LLMConfig keys be added to that file's _clean fixture (monkeypatch.delenv list). If this story adds new config keys to LLMConfig (as it should per the first finding), the same _clean fixture update is required — otherwise the new env vars leak between tests.
findings.location: Files section (omits tests/unit/test_config_llm.py); 95a5 AC: 'test_config_llm.py's _clean fixture lists them'
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'REBAR_LLM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_S', 'REBAR_LLM_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S', 'REBAR_LLM_AGENTIC_REQUEST_LIMIT', 'REBAR_LLM_AGENTIC_TOOL_CALLS_LIMIT' to the monkeypatch.delenv block in test_config_llm.py's _clean fixture, mirroring how 95a5 handles its keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly defer with rationale) that exercises the httpx read-timeout path end-to-end against the real transport: either a live integration test (even in a separate `make test-integration` target, using a minimal model call with an artificially low timeout against a real or local proxy endpoint) that confirms `ReadTimeout` is raised and classified, OR state explicitly in the plan why live timeout testing is impractical (e.g., 'inducing a real API timeout is non-deterministic; deferred to production monitoring') so the deferral is traceable.
findings.finding: All timeout-firing acceptance criteria (ACs 1–3) are verified exclusively via injected offline mocks (FunctionModel / sleeping tool); no AC exercises the httpx read-timeout or tool-timeout against the real LLM transport as it will run in production, and no explicit deferral rationale is stated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC3; Scope — 'own timeout probe' using FunctionModel

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the observability channel for each timeout-event classification (e.g., a structured log entry with `{event: 'llm_read_timeout', elapsed_s: ...}` and/or a counter metric) so operators can distinguish timeout-bounded failures from model errors and tune the config defaults.
findings.finding: New timeout-event failures (read timeout firing, tool timeout firing, step cap hit) have no specified observability instrumentation — no log entry, metric, or alert channel is described for operators to distinguish a healthy completed run from a run that was bounded by a timeout mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC lines 1–3; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: 'Each timeout-event classification (read timeout, tool timeout, step cap) emits a structured log entry at WARN level with the event type, elapsed time, and config threshold that was hit.' Wire this in runner.py's exception-handling block around run_sync.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan section (or AC) that names the exception types each timeout mechanism raises, states how runner.py wraps or re-raises them, and acknowledges any existing callers whose error-handling must be updated.
findings.finding: The plan says hung requests and hung tools produce 'classified failures' but never specifies what exception type propagates to existing callers of run_sync, nor whether the runner's current error-handling wraps these new timeout exceptions — leaving the behavior-change impact on callers unacknowledged.
findings.location: What section ('surfaces a classified failure') / Scope / AC lines 2–3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that ticket 2eca exists (or create it) before relying on it as the sole ADR owner in AC5; if it doesn't exist, either create the stub ticket or assign ADR ownership to this story directly.
findings.finding: The plan references 'story 2eca' as the sole ADR owner but this ticket ID does not appear in the codebase, the .rebar/ cache, or any reachable ticket reference. If 2eca does not exist as a ticket, the delegation in AC5 has no live target — the ADR content would fall into a gap with no owner.
findings.location: AC5: 'RECORDED by story 2eca, the sole ADR owner'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
